title: D.C.

jokes.text: Selina: Oh, God. I hate impeachments. They're so '90s.
jokes.setup: O'Brien gravely warns about a cloud of suspicion hanging over the administration with potential cover-ups and lies
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the constitutional crisis as merely dated and passé, like a fashion trend
jokes.explanation: Perfectly calibrated Selina character comedy — her self-absorbed shallowness reducing an impeachment to a fashion statement. The '90s reference (Clintonesque) adds a layer for the politically savvy. Economy is excellent. Genuinely funny and character-specific.

jokes.text: Jonah: No, I just have a 'don't ask don't tell' policy about the things that I know. Or that I don't know.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to recover from being exposed as ignorant
jokes.punchline: His 'clarification' only deepens how meaningless and self-undermining his statement is
jokes.explanation: Jonah digs his own grave deeper. Funny but a little loose — the second half slightly undercuts the impact of the first. Still good character work showing his attempt to sound savvy while admitting ignorance.

jokes.text: Jonah, don't talk, don't stay. You need to fuck off and go back to Westworld.
jokes.setup: Jonah continues to irritate Selina with his pointless presence
jokes.punchline: 'Westworld' — a dismissive, absurd geographical insult implying Jonah is from somewhere fictional and irrelevant
jokes.explanation: Veep's signature insult comedy at a strong level. 'Westworld' is a left-field specific choice that elevates a dismissal into something memorably weird. Economy is strong — the insult does a lot of work in very few words.

jokes.text: Three fucks, you're out.
jokes.setup: Selina has said 'fuck off' three times to Jonah in rapid succession
jokes.punchline: Baseball rule applied to profanity — three strikes, you're out becomes three fucks, you're out
jokes.explanation: Beautifully compact. The rule of three fuck-offs is self-aware and the baseball punchline lands with perfect economy. Highly quotable, shows Selina's wit even in dismissal. One of the better jokes of the episode.

jokes.text: Ma'am, you have my deepest, deepest sympathies. / Okay, well, I'm not dead.
jokes.setup: Selina announces she's leaving the ticket — a political setback, not a death
jokes.punchline: Gary's overwrought condolences treat it like a funeral; Selina points out she's still alive
jokes.explanation: Good Gary character moment — his excessive emotional attunement to Selina causes comic mismatch. The beat is quick and well-structured. Standard sitcom misunderstanding elevated by the specific characters involved.

jokes.text: Dan immediately starts making calls to other employers the moment Selina announces she's leaving — while still in the room.
jokes.setup: Selina tells her team they may need to look for other jobs but says no one is thinking about that right now
jokes.punchline: Dan is already on the phone networking before she's finished speaking
jokes.explanation: Pitch-perfect Dan character comedy. His shameless self-interest is so acute he can't even fake loyalty for the duration of the announcement. The visual confirmation of exactly what Selina just said won't happen makes this a great reaction beat with strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: Well, your devotion to this job... it's just inspiring, Dan.
jokes.setup: Dan is visibly on the phone networking for new jobs while Selina is still announcing her plans
jokes.punchline: Selina calls out his naked disloyalty with maximally dry sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Clean sarcastic punctuation to joke #7. Works as a pair — the visual gag of Dan on the phone and then Selina's acid response. Good economy and timing though not independently exceptional.

jokes.text: He's not getting a call. He's making a call, right? / Hi. Yes. Can you please tell Governor Chung that Dan Egan is ready for the Chung chat?
jokes.setup: Dan pretends to receive an important call; Amy sees through it
jokes.punchline: Dan confirms Amy's suspicion immediately and audaciously — he's already pitching himself to a rival politician
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'maybe he's getting a call' to him loudly announcing 'Chung chat' is wonderfully brazen. Alliterative 'Chung chat' is a small but effective comic detail. Dan's sociopathic audacity is well-deployed.

jokes.text: No, it means I have absolutely nothing to do. / I meant for her. / Still, this might mean that we could spend more time together. / Let's take it one day at a time. Sweet Jesus.
jokes.setup: Dan expresses concern that Selina leaving is 'such a shame' — Amy assumes he means for Selina
jokes.punchline: Dan meant it's a shame for himself (no work to do), then pivots to suggesting they spend more time together; Amy's horrified 'Sweet Jesus' is the punchline
jokes.explanation: Three-beat escalating joke — Dan's narcissism, his creepy pivot to intimacy, Amy's visceral rejection. The 'Sweet Jesus' lands well as the cap. Good cringe comedy in the Amy/Dan dynamic.

jokes.text: Yes, Congressman Kosynski. Or should I say mentor Kosynski? The father figure my own dad was too weird and distant to be.
jokes.setup: Dan is flattering an older congressman to secure a job
jokes.punchline: He over-shares about his 'weird and distant' father, transforming professional flattery into uncomfortable psychological revelation
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep overshare — Dan's sociopathic charm-offensive accidentally revealing real psychological damage. The specificity of 'weird and distant' (not just 'absent') is a good character detail. Funny and unsettling.

jokes.text: Like that night that you stayed over and we read the papers the next morning? That was really nice. And I thought, yeah, that is something that I could do for the rest of my life.
jokes.setup: Dan is establishing his relationship with the congressman as a mentor
jokes.punchline: He describes a night the congressman stayed over with oddly intimate, almost romantic language — reading papers together sounds like a post-coital morning
jokes.explanation: The unintentional homoerotic subtext of Dan's description — 'stayed over,' 'rest of my life' — is peak Veep cringe. The audience sees it even if Dan doesn't. Good rewatch value as you catch the full implication.

jokes.text: You know, you're gonna get bedsores.
jokes.setup: Dan says he wants to read papers and sleep forever — a romantic notion
jokes.punchline: The congressman responds to the literal implications with clinical practicality: bedsores
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy — two words puncture Dan's sentimentality with medical reality. The deadpan absurdity of responding to emotional flattery with bedsores is quintessential Veep. Very clean joke.

jokes.text: I move as slowly as a Mississippi detective investigating the murder of a young black man.
jokes.setup: Ben asks Will how he moves, setting up a comic self-description
jokes.punchline: Will's answer is a shockingly dark racial justice joke about police indifference to Black victims
jokes.explanation: One of the most startling jokes in the episode. It works because the setup is completely innocuous (how do you move?) and the answer is a specific, politically charged image that recontextualizes a throwaway question into social commentary. The specificity — 'Mississippi detective,' 'murder of a young black man' — makes this feel genuinely subversive rather than cheap. This is the kind of joke that makes a room go silent and then erupt. Highly quotable and controversial in the best way. Justifies 8+ score easily.

jokes.text: Hey, ma'am, you know you're gonna break the president's heart with this news. / Wish I could break his spine. But I can't, 'cause it's made of Jell-O.
jokes.setup: Gary offers a sentimental thought about POTUS's feelings
jokes.punchline: Selina's violent wish is immediately undercut by the president's spinelessness — she can't even wish him harm properly because he's too weak
jokes.explanation: Two-step joke with excellent construction: the aggressive wish followed immediately by self-deflation because the target is too pathetic to properly despise. 'Spine made of Jell-O' is a vivid, fresh metaphor. Highly quotable and pitch-perfect Selina character work. Justifies the 8 score.

jokes.text: Are you finishing my sentence or are you telling me that again? / Both.
jokes.setup: Ben begins to say 'fuck off' (completing Selina's earlier pattern) right as Selina cuts him off asking if he's finishing her sentence or swearing at her
jokes.punchline: 'Both' — perfectly economical, Ben owns both interpretations simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke. 'Both' is the ideal one-word response — maximally economical, covers all bases, is funny as a callback to the Jonah fuck-off sequence, and captures Ben's weary pragmatism perfectly. Structure is immaculate: the setup creates a genuine question and the answer collapses both possibilities. The economy score of 10 is earned — you cannot say this joke in fewer words.

jokes.text: I really don't have any need for you, Mike. You're kind of obsolete. You're like a Viking stuck in time.
jokes.setup: Mike pitches himself as a press guy with 'excellent contacts'
jokes.punchline: 'Viking stuck in time' — an oddly specific, evocative insult for someone outdated
jokes.explanation: Good Veep-style insult — the unexpectedness of 'Viking stuck in time' as a metaphor for obsolescence is characteristic of the show's creative put-downs. Not one of the best but solidly above average.

jokes.text: Maybe you can hook me up with Charlize Theron. / Funny, but I'm being serious, sir. / No, I'm being serious, too. I'd like to fuck her.
jokes.setup: Mike claims he has contacts with 'very high up people'
jokes.punchline: The congressman takes 'high up people' to mean celebrities he'd like to sleep with — and then doubles down that he's not joking
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is clean — Mike's protestations that he's serious are met with the congressman confirming his own entirely different seriousness. The pivot from political contacts to sexual desire is funny and the deadpan insistence sells it.

jokes.text: Why'd you leave anyway? You get stuck with your dick in the veep's decaf?
jokes.setup: Mike says they 'all need new challenges' as a reason for leaving
jokes.punchline: The congressman suggests Mike had an affair with Selina and was forced out
jokes.explanation: 'Dick in the veep's decaf' is a vivid, offensive, and memorable formulation. The word 'decaf' does a lot of heavy lifting — it's not just crude, it's specifically degrading (decaf = weak, secondary, fake). Good economy and shock value.

jokes.text: Holy shit. She's leaving the ticket. / No, absolutely not. We had a fucking deal.
jokes.setup: Mike accidentally confirms Selina is leaving the ticket while trying to deny it
jokes.punchline: His increasingly desperate denials make it more obvious — the congressman has already pieced it together
jokes.explanation: Standard 'the denial confirms it' sitcom beat, executed competently. Mike's fumbling is consistent with his character but the joke itself is fairly familiar territory.

jokes.text: Why don't you fucking kill yourself, Will?
jokes.setup: Will hovers uselessly in the background during a tense negotiation
jokes.punchline: The congressman's reaction to Will's presence is maximally disproportionate — from zero to 'kill yourself'
jokes.explanation: Veep-typical profane overreaction. Shock value helps but it's a fairly cheap laugh by the show's standards — the specificity and wit that make the best Veep insults aren't fully here.

jokes.text: Your news is probably more like a page six, Kardashian crap news.
jokes.setup: Selina and Ben compete about who has bigger news
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses Ben's news as celebrity gossip — ironic given her own political situation
jokes.explanation: Kardashian reference feels slightly dated even for 2013 and the joke is fairly generic. The competitive news-comparing setup is fun but the punchline doesn't elevate it. Functional but unremarkable.

jokes.text: He drank the poisoned Kool-Aid from my very big, blue mug.
jokes.setup: Ben is setting up that POTUS has agreed to step down — framing himself as having persuaded him
jokes.punchline: The 'poisoned Kool-Aid' metaphor is extended absurdly with the specific detail of his 'very big, blue mug' — making mass-suicide imagery cozy and domestic
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'very big, blue mug' attached to a Jonestown metaphor is genuinely funny — it domesticates horror. Ben's weary, matter-of-fact delivery of dark material is well-served.

jokes.text: Are you serious? / No, I'm Joan fucking Rivers. Of course I'm serious.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if Ben is serious about POTUS not running
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'Joan fucking Rivers' denial — using the comedian's name as shorthand for theatrical insincerity
jokes.explanation: Using Joan Rivers as the avatar of 'obviously not serious' is apt and specific. The profanity placement is perfect ('Joan fucking Rivers'). Clean, quick, effective.

jokes.text: Because he couldn't stomach the look -- like that, just like that -- of unbridled joy.
jokes.setup: Selina asks why POTUS didn't tell her himself
jokes.punchline: Ben points at Selina's expression of barely-contained ecstasy as the answer — and she's doing it right now as he explains
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural joke — the explanation IS demonstrated simultaneously, making it self-proving comedy. 'Like that, just like that' implies he's catching her mid-gleeful-expression. The real-time visual confirmation of his point is sophisticated comedy writing that rewards attention. Justifies 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Ben, I am crying very quietly on the inside. / While in your mind you're doing pirouettes on his grave.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to perform appropriate sadness about POTUS stepping down
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately translates her fake grief into her actual triumphant fantasy
jokes.explanation: 'Pirouettes on his grave' is a brilliantly specific image — not just celebration but triumphant, elegant, performative celebration. The contrast between Selina's stated 'quiet crying' and Ben's vision of joyful pirouetting is perfectly chosen. Strong line.

jokes.text: While in your mind you're doing pirouettes on his grave.
jokes.setup: Ben responds to Selina's fake grief.
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'pirouettes' — not just dancing but the most jubilant, graceful, and theatrical form of dancing — on POTUS's political grave.
jokes.explanation: The pirouette image is more elegant and funnier than 'dancing' would be — it implies a very deliberate, practiced, almost ceremonial joy. Strong word choice.

jokes.text: Well, it's not the job that's depressing. Life is depressing.
jokes.setup: Ben and Selina discuss whether the job is depressing now that she's leaving
jokes.punchline: Ben's existential deflection — it's not the job, it's everything
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ben line. His nihilistic world-weariness is his defining characteristic and 'life is depressing' as a casual reframe is the kind of thing only Ben could say with this delivery. High economy, high character integration.

jokes.text: Selina alone after receiving the news, suppressing a squeal, then whispering 'Yes! Fuck! Gosh!' in rapid succession
jokes.setup: Selina has maintained composure while with Ben — now she's alone
jokes.punchline: The dam breaks in the most Selina way possible: suppressed squeal, then a triumphant 'Yes!', immediately followed by 'Fuck! Gosh!' — the swearing then the self-correction showing her code-switching even when alone
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The sequence 'Yes! Fuck! Gosh!' is comedic gold — the involuntary profanity followed by the self-correcting 'Gosh' reveals that even in her most private moment, Selina Meyer is performing a version of herself. The physical comedy of the suppressed squeal plus the verbal sequence is perfectly constructed. This is genuine character revelation through comedy. Justifies high scores.

jokes.text: We'd like to see the vice president as a matter of some urgency. / No.
jokes.setup: A congressman and aide approach Sue demanding to see Selina urgently
jokes.punchline: Sue's monosyllabic refusal — no hedging, no excuse, just 'No'
jokes.explanation: Sue's 'No' is maximally economical Sue comedy — she gatekeeps with complete serenity and zero explanation. The single word against the elaborate urgent request is the perfect power dynamic joke. Maximum economy score warranted.

jokes.text: Fuck this, I'm going in the side door. / You do and Secret Service might break your neck. / That would be a tragedy.
jokes.setup: A congressman threatens to go around Sue's obstruction
jokes.punchline: Sue's 'that would be a tragedy' — delivered with zero affect, implying the opposite
jokes.explanation: Sue's deadpan 'that would be a tragedy' when describing the congressman's potential neck-breaking is sharp irony — she clearly doesn't think it would be a tragedy at all. Good Sue character work.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm burning calories with the old anxiety diet. You know, the anxiet. / If you want to get some cardio exercise, you have to have a heart.
jokes.setup: Roger says he's on the 'anxiety diet' getting cardio
jokes.punchline: Selina says to get cardio you need a heart — implying he has none
jokes.explanation: Good two-part joke — the 'anxiet' truncation is a small funny detail, and Selina's heart-cardio retort is clean if not spectacular. The insult works because it's accurate to his character. Solid mid-tier Veep joke.

jokes.text: Where did you hear this rumor? / Oh, I heard it from the Gingerhead Man. The gash with the stash. McLintock.
jokes.setup: Selina asks where the rumor about her leaving the ticket came from
jokes.punchline: Roger's elaborate nickname sequence for McLintock — 'Gingerhead Man,' then 'the gash with the stash'
jokes.explanation: 'The gash with the stash' is an inventive, alliterative, deeply rude nickname. The escalating reveal — 'Gingerhead Man' sets it up, 'the gash with the stash' delivers, then the actual name lands as anticlimax. Good wordplay and Veep-specific creative insult construction.

jokes.text: I am absolutely running in two years. And then four years after that, reelection, baby. / What do you mean reelection?
jokes.setup: Selina is reassuring Roger she's staying on the ticket
jokes.punchline: Her slip — saying 'reelection' when she means 'election,' revealing she's already mentally positioned as the incumbent president
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-revealing slip — Selina has mentally already won the presidency and is running as incumbent in her own mind. It's both funny and deeply revealing about her character. The way Roger immediately catches it makes the beat land perfectly.

jokes.text: It's like saying flammable or inflammable. It's the same thing. / I smell a fucking rat. / Oh, stop it. / I'll tell you what it is, too. It's this whole administration. And I'm gonna light a fire under the whole thing. Then we'll find out whether it's flammable or inflammable.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to explain away her 'reelection' slip by comparing it to flammable/inflammable
jokes.punchline: Roger calls her a rat, then callbacks to the flammable/inflammable analogy to make a political threat — the metaphor she chose to deflect him becomes a weapon against her
jokes.explanation: One of the best constructed jokes in the episode. Selina tries to escape via linguistic analogy; Roger immediately weaponizes her own analogy against her as a threat. The callback within the same exchange is exceptionally tight writing. The conceit of 'we'll find out whether it's flammable or inflammable' as a political threat is both funny and genuinely menacing. Highly quotable. Fully justifies 8+ scores.

jokes.text: Come on, Wilbo Faggins, let's go.
jokes.setup: Roger is about to leave after confronting Selina
jokes.punchline: His nickname for his aide Will is a profane Lord of the Rings mashup — 'Wilbo Faggins' (Bilbo Baggins + Will + 'fag')
jokes.explanation: The portmanteau is inventive and the LOTR specificity is funny — but the slur element (Faggins) dates it and feels cheap compared to other Veep insults. The craft in the construction is real but the earned score suffers for the lazy shock component.

jokes.text: You like to have sex and you like to travel? / Yes, ma'am. / Then you can fuck off.
jokes.setup: Classic 'go fuck yourself' joke setup disguised as two friendly questions
jokes.punchline: 'Then you can fuck off' — combining 'sex' and 'travel' to equal 'fuck off'
jokes.explanation: This is a very old joke (classic British 'go fuck yourself' setup) repurposed for Selina-Jonah dynamic. The originality suffers for it being a well-known gag, but the execution and character context are good. Functional and gets a laugh.

jokes.text: But I shall be right here if you need me. Like the mighty oak that stands for-- ma'am.
jokes.setup: Jonah is dismissed and tries to give a dignified exit speech
jokes.punchline: He begins a grandiose oak tree metaphor and is cut off mid-sentence, reducing him to just saying 'ma'am' — total deflation
jokes.explanation: Classic Jonah joke — his pretension completely deflated mid-sentence. The 'mighty oak' setup is exactly right for Jonah's self-mythologizing, and the abrupt truncation to 'ma'am' is perfectly structured. His wounded dignity is the punchline. Strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah! That's awesome. I did not see that coming. Selina Meyer, she deals 'em fire. Bam! Boom! Let's go to the fucking moon!
jokes.setup: Dan has already been caught calling Governor Chung's office to defect
jokes.punchline: Dan immediately pivots to enthusiastic loyalty — 'Bam! Boom! Let's go to the fucking moon!' — with zero self-awareness about his earlier betrayal
jokes.explanation: The callback to Dan's instant defection makes his now-instant re-loyalty hysterically funny. The juxtaposition is everything — he was calling Chung 10 minutes ago. 'Let's go to the fucking moon' is peak sociopathic enthusiasm. High character integration because only Dan could this seamlessly switch sides and commit this hard.

jokes.text: I was this close to living on my boat.
jokes.setup: Everyone is celebrating Selina's presidential run announcement
jokes.punchline: Mike's contribution to the celebration is about the boat he almost got to live on — revealing his political career is largely an obstacle to his retirement dream
jokes.explanation: This is a perfect character joke. While everyone celebrates a historic moment, Mike is mourning his boat. It's both hilarious and deeply sad — Mike never wanted any of this. The economy is exceptional. Sets up the boat running gag for the episode's later payoff. Justifies 8+ for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Can you give me some time with my core team? Um, Br-- Br- / Ed. / Bre-- Ed. / Of course.
jokes.setup: Selina wants private time with her inner circle and begins to dismiss the newer staffers
jokes.punchline: She doesn't know Ed's name — she gets stuck on a wrong letter twice before he supplies it himself
jokes.explanation: Good Selina moment — her casual disregard for people she considers peripheral is expressed through not knowing their names. The repeated failed attempt at 'Br-' before he supplies 'Ed' is funny in delivery. Classic Veep power dynamic comedy.

jokes.text: I feel as though I may have missed something. If you need to fill me in on that-- / Hey, what happened in there? / They're celebrating because you weren't in there.
jokes.setup: Ed and Jonah are both excluded from Selina's inner circle meeting
jokes.punchline: Amy confirms the celebration is specifically because they were excluded
jokes.explanation: Amy's blunt confirmation is perfectly delivered — she doesn't even soften it. The idea that the party's joy is partly 'you're not here' is both cruel and plausible in this world.

jokes.text: Uh, I've accepted a number of posts. / Did you say a number? / Currently four.
jokes.setup: Selina tells the team they don't need to look for other jobs anymore
jokes.punchline: Dan has already accepted four posts — not one, FOUR — while the meeting was happening
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'I made a call' to 'currently four posts accepted' is masterful. The word 'currently' implies the number might still be growing. This is exceptional Dan character comedy — his sociopathic efficiency in job-hunting is treated as perfectly reasonable behavior. The callback thread of Dan's defection pays off here. Justifies 8+ clearly.

jokes.text: Wow. You're gonna cancel them, right? / Oh, clearly. Yeah. Oh, yeah.
jokes.setup: Selina assumes Dan will obviously cancel his four accepted jobs
jokes.punchline: Dan's enthusiastic agreement is clearly insincere — his 'Oh, clearly' has all the conviction of someone who will absolutely not be canceling anything
jokes.explanation: Good beat continuing the Dan thread — his transparent lying is funny because Selina either doesn't notice or doesn't care. Functions as comedic punctuation to joke #44.

jokes.text: I'm going into business with Dana and we're doing D&G Cheese Overseas.
jokes.setup: Gary reveals his alternative job
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'D&G Cheese Overseas' — a cheese business with international scope — is inherently absurd coming from Gary, the most DC-enmeshed person on the team
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the mismatch — Gary, who exists entirely to serve Selina, has somehow developed an international cheese business. 'And it's overseas' as Amy's incredulous echo of the most baffling part is a good beat.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. You're choosing dead milk over me?
jokes.setup: Gary explains he's going into cheese with his fiancée Dana
jokes.punchline: Selina's characterization of cheese as 'dead milk' — grossly reductive, slightly horrified
jokes.explanation: 'Dead milk' is one of the best single jokes in the episode. It's surprising, specific, biological, and tells you everything about Selina's character — her inability to frame anything except in relation to herself, and her disdain for the domestic. Highly quotable, memorable, and perfectly economical. Fully justifies 8+ scores.

jokes.text: Like I'm Marilyn Monroe, just JFK the fuck out of me.
jokes.setup: Selina wants her science fair speech made more presidential
jokes.punchline: She uses Marilyn Monroe/JFK as her model for presidential charisma — an affair/assassination reference used casually as a metaphor for speech coaching
jokes.explanation: The construction is brilliant — 'JFK the fuck out of me' as a verb, using the most loaded political-celebrity relationship in American history as a casual speech coaching metaphor. It's inappropriate, specific, and only Selina could say it with this blithe confidence. Highly quotable. Economy is excellent. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: No, it's all about crazy money, Ame. He's got the crazy money and I'm crazy enough to go get it. / As long as it's strictly business.
jokes.setup: Selina is calling her ex Andrew for campaign fundraising
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'as long as it's strictly business' implies it absolutely won't be; Selina's 'crazy money / I'm crazy enough' inadvertently rhymes madness with her own self-description
jokes.explanation: Amy's dry 'strictly business' is the real joke here — the understatement of romantic history. Selina's 'crazy' word repetition is a minor comic detail. Functional, good Amy-Selina dynamic.

jokes.text: Dana: Oh, there he is. Hey, quit being so hot. You're gonna melt all my cheese.
jokes.setup: Gary arrives at Dana's cheese display
jokes.punchline: Dana's flirtatious 'you're gonna melt my cheese' — using the cheese context to create an accidental double entendre
jokes.explanation: Good situational wordplay — the cheese setting makes the 'melt' flirtation both literal and metaphorical. Dana's ebullient character is established quickly and the cheese-flirting is funny.

jokes.text: You look like you did when I asked you to talk dirty. You were just like, 'I'm in you right now.'
jokes.setup: Gary looks nervous and uncomfortable; Dana reads his expression
jokes.punchline: His attempt at dirty talk was literally just a clinical, biological description — 'I'm in you right now' — maximum cringe
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Gary's 'dirty talk' is so earnestly wrong — 'I'm in you right now' is accurate, clinical, and deeply unsexy. It's perfectly calibrated to his character: helpful, precise, well-meaning, completely missing the point. The specificity of this line makes it extraordinarily quotable. The cringe is earned because we understand Gary completely. Fully justifies high scores.

jokes.text: The Pecorino Duo Grande sampler. Shaved and grated.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to handle the situation; Dana continues to try to integrate cheese into everything
jokes.punchline: Dana's specification of 'shaved and grated' — bringing the level of cheese detail into a moment of emotional tension
jokes.explanation: The specificity of cheese detail in an emotionally loaded scene is funny. Dana's obliviousness to Gary's crisis while focused on cheese varieties is good character comedy but not fully developed yet.

jokes.text: Sorry, not black truffle. I meant white truffle. / That's next to the black truffle, okay. / What the fuck is wrong with you, Brett? This is not a big place, is it? It's not a big place.
jokes.setup: Dana is simultaneously conducting a relationship ultimatum with Gary AND running a cheese stall
jokes.punchline: A customer's cheese confusion derails the emotional conversation; Dana's frustration erupts at the customer mid-relationship crisis
jokes.explanation: The structural comedy of an emotionally significant conversation being interrupted by cheese logistics is excellent Veep-style bathos. 'What the fuck is wrong with you, Brett? This is not a big place' captures Dana's bottled frustration exploding sideways at a cheese customer. The interweaving of the relationship drama with truffle navigation is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Would you guys like some space? / Yeah! / No! Stay right where you are.
jokes.setup: A bystander offers to give the arguing couple space
jokes.punchline: Dana and Gary answer simultaneously but oppositely — Dana wants space (to fight), Gary doesn't (because that's worse)
jokes.explanation: Efficient comedic beat — the simultaneous opposite answers reveal exactly where each character is emotionally. Clean structure, good character differentiation.

jokes.text: You know, listen, just ignore that earlier me. The 'Little House on the Prairie' bullshit.
jokes.setup: Selina is recalibrating with Amy now that she's running for president
jokes.punchline: 'Little House on the Prairie' — Selina's shorthand for her brief moment of domesticity/sentimentality, treated as something to be disowned
jokes.explanation: 'Little House on the Prairie' as Selina's self-mocking epithet for her vulnerability is perfectly chosen — it's old, wholesome, domestic, and completely antithetical to her identity. Good economy.

jokes.text: I am right behind/beside you. You, Chief of Staff to the president, me, chief of sexiness and spooning.
jokes.setup: Amy expresses her loyalty to Selina's campaign
jokes.punchline: Amy articulates her career aspiration (Chief of Staff) alongside a domestic dream role ('chief of sexiness and spooning') — revealing the tension in her between ambition and wanting intimacy
jokes.explanation: Amy jokes about her softer side while maintaining the Chief of Staff ambition. 'Chief of sexiness and spooning' is a funny bureaucratic title applied to romance. Reveals character well and plays on the Dan/Amy dynamic.

jokes.text: Accidental dick move. I had to write Selina's speech for the science fair so fast that I think I plagiarized a speech that I wrote for Chung.
jokes.setup: Dan knocks and Amy welcomes him into her confidential conversation with Selina
jokes.punchline: 'Accidental dick move' as Dan's self-description; followed by the revelation that he gave Selina a speech he'd already sold to Chung
jokes.explanation: 'Accidental dick move' is a great formulation — it acknowledges the harm without accepting blame ('accidental'). The plagiarism reveal is perfectly Dan: not malicious exactly, just catastrophically self-serving and careless.

jokes.text: It's not about what America means to me. It's what I mean to America. Because America would be nothing without Americans. / Jesus, you wrote this shit? / Yeah, it's like a noun-verb gumbo.
jokes.setup: They watch the plagiarized speech on YouTube — it's revealed to be speechwriting nonsense
jokes.punchline: 'Noun-verb gumbo' — Dan's self-aware critique of his own terrible writing
jokes.explanation: Double-barreled joke: first, the speech itself is an excellent parody of empty political rhetoric (Americans = America, circular tautology), and then Dan's self-aware 'noun-verb gumbo' labels his own hack work with perfect precision. Both parts are strong. 'Noun-verb gumbo' is highly quotable and a genuinely sharp description of bad speechwriting. Fully justifies 8+.

jokes.text: So it's the fictitious girl. I gave her the same girl. Although I did change it from Juanita to Anita.
jokes.setup: Dan reveals the details of the plagiarism
jokes.punchline: His solution to the duplicate plagiarized girl was to change her name from Juanita to Anita — the bare minimum, almost identical names
jokes.explanation: The Juanita → Anita 'solution' is perfect — barely different, still clearly Latin, still fictional, total non-fix. The escalation of incompetence is well-structured. Dan's confidence in this minimal change is classic character comedy. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Oh, that's a brilliant plan. Why don't you change America to Shmamerica? Yeah, I'm calling Mike.
jokes.setup: Dan defends his Juanita/Anita name change as a fix
jokes.punchline: Amy's sarcasm peaks with 'Shmamerica' — a perfect reductio ad absurdum of Dan's approach
jokes.explanation: 'Shmamerica' is a brilliantly specific comic escalation — the Yiddish-inflected 'shm-' prefix applied to America takes Dan's minimal-change approach to its logical extreme. Good economy and Amy's exasperation is perfectly channeled through the joke.

jokes.text: Well, from up here, they look like ants. / ( Mike chuckles ) / That's a joke. / Because they are ants.
jokes.setup: Selina looks at an ant kibbutz science exhibit and makes an observation
jokes.punchline: She explains that her 'they look like ants' joke is a joke — then immediately undercuts it by noting they literally are ants
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Selina makes a standard observation joke, flags it as a joke (already funny), then deflates both the joke and her own explanation with 'because they are ants.' The meta-commentary on her own comedy falling apart in real time is sophisticated. The structure is three-layered. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: What? I didn't say anything to that crusty ass clown. / I might have mixed up a couple words, but...
jokes.setup: Ben calls to say Furlong is spreading that POTUS will quit based on something Selina said
jokes.punchline: Amy immediately admits she 'might have mixed up a couple words' — a massive understatement for accidentally confirming a political bombshell
jokes.explanation: 'Might have mixed up a couple words' as a descriptor for leaking a presidential decision is classic Veep understatement. 'Crusty ass clown' for Furlong is also a good insult. The scene's comedy rests on both.

jokes.text: So now our spineless, flip-floppy fuckbag is staying.
jokes.setup: POTUS can't announce he's not running because it'll look like he was pushed out
jokes.punchline: 'Spineless, flip-floppy fuckbag' — an alliterative insult perfectly calibrated to the president's character
jokes.explanation: The alliteration of 'spineless, flip-floppy fuckbag' is musically satisfying and the accusation is character-specific — the president IS flip-floppy. The F-alliteration is perfectly placed. Strong Veep insult construction.

jokes.text: I've got to get out of here before I set fire to one of these nerds.
jokes.setup: Selina is losing her mind at the science fair as her political situation collapses
jokes.punchline: Her frustration focuses on the most innocent possible targets — science fair kids — with violent imagery
jokes.explanation: Selina's displaced aggression onto science fair children is darkly funny. 'Set fire to one of these nerds' is vivid and specific. Good dark character comedy.

jokes.text: Don't fuck this up. This is a public relations nightmare waiting to happen.
jokes.setup: Selina dumps the science fair on Mike as she rushes out, telling him to cover for her
jokes.punchline: She tells him not to fuck it up in the same breath as calling it a 'nightmare waiting to happen' — which is exactly what will happen because she left
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina irony — she creates the nightmare by leaving and blames Mike for the inevitable result. Competent but not one of the stronger moments.

jokes.text: DC's current go-to place for these off-the-record meets. / Good choice. Under the radar as we used to say in the military.
jokes.setup: Dan meets Chung in what he presents as a savvy location choice
jokes.punchline: Chung's 'as we used to say in the military' applied to a common expression reveals his constant need to militarize everything
jokes.explanation: Chung's military-credentialing applied to 'under the radar' — an expression everyone uses — exposes his compulsive self-mythologizing. Dan's 'yeah, I think other people say that, too' is the perfect deadpan reality check.

jokes.text: Yeah, I think other people say that, too.
jokes.setup: Chung claims 'under the radar' as military parlance
jokes.punchline: Dan gently deflates the military appropriation with maximum understatement
jokes.explanation: The economy here is excellent — Dan's brief understatement does a lot of work deflating Chung's pomposity without overplaying it. Short and effective.

jokes.text: That's the worst kind of friendly fire. The unfriendly kind.
jokes.setup: Chung describes political opponents turning against the president as 'friendly fire'
jokes.punchline: 'The worst kind of friendly fire — the unfriendly kind' — a tautological reversal that exposes the oxymoron in 'friendly fire'
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay — Chung's military-speak collapses on itself. 'Friendly fire' is an oxymoron, and 'the unfriendly kind' as a clarification is both obvious and funny. Clean construction, well-performed.

jokes.text: After all, you didn't really meet anyone named Juanita who said that. / No, I did. As I recall, it went something like... ( Spanish accent ) 'Mr. Ching, I come to America to work. I send money home, but life here is so hard for me.' / I didn't know you did voices.
jokes.setup: Dan points out that the Juanita story is fabricated
jokes.punchline: Chung doubles down with a full-on fake Spanish accent performance of the fictional immigrant woman, adding the racism of mispronouncing his own name as 'Ching'
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary cringe comedy. Chung performs an improvised fake Spanish accent to legitimize a fictional immigrant woman who never existed, while also getting his own (Asian) name wrong as 'Ching.' The layers of cluelessness are precisely calibrated. Dan's 'I didn't know you did voices' is a perfect deadpan response to an unhinged moment. The character work is exceptional — Chung's desperate political self-justification through performance is both believable and deeply uncomfortable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Jesus, the inside of his head must be like an Escher drawing.
jokes.setup: POTUS keeps flip-flopping on his decision to run or not run
jokes.punchline: An Escher drawing — infinitely recursive, no exit, spatially impossible — as a metaphor for POTUS's decision-making
jokes.explanation: The Escher reference is genuinely well-chosen — not just 'confusing' but specifically 'impossible geometry that goes nowhere and folds back on itself.' Amy's line shows intelligence and is character-appropriate. A better-than-average metaphor in a show full of good ones. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: Oh, good, more hurt and disappointment.
jokes.setup: Sue announces Andrew is here to see Selina after her latest setback
jokes.punchline: Selina greets the news of her ex-husband's arrival as another guaranteed source of misery
jokes.explanation: Short but effective. The certainty of Andrew = hurt and disappointment is funny and telling. Good economy.

jokes.text: You think you get to decide on whether or not I hire you? There are plenty of Dans out there, Dan. Look around.
jokes.setup: Dan tells Chung he's staying with Selina after all
jokes.punchline: 'There are plenty of Dans out there, Dan' — the name repetition in the face of Dan's self-regard is deflating and funny
jokes.explanation: The 'plenty of Dans' construction nicely undercuts Dan's massive ego — he's fungible, just another Dan. The specificity of addressing him by name at the end of the sentence drives it home.

jokes.text: Hey, don't call me kid, okay? I mean, A: you're not Jimmy Cagney, all right? And B: we're practically the same fucking age.
jokes.setup: Chung dismisses Dan with 'Sorry, kid'
jokes.punchline: Dan's double-pronged objection — A: wrong genre (Chung isn't a gangster movie), B: they're the same age — both deflect the condescension
jokes.explanation: The A:/B: structuring of the objection is funny (bureaucratic formality applied to personal offense), and the Jimmy Cagney reference is specific and good. The age point is the better half — it deflates Chung's authority more completely.

jokes.text: Then it must really hurt to know how much more I've achieved in life. Decorated veteran. President-in-waiting.
jokes.setup: Dan points out they're the same age to counter Chung's condescension
jokes.punchline: Chung responds with a resume punch — 'decorated veteran, president-in-waiting' — delivered as if reading from a plaque about himself
jokes.explanation: Chung's self-mythologizing reaches its peak — he literally lists his own credentials as if reading a Wikipedia header. 'President-in-waiting' is a great delusional title. Good character comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, okay. Do you see this? See this right here? You know what this is? This is me playing 'Fuck You' on the world's biggest cello.
jokes.setup: Chung dismisses Dan as less accomplished
jokes.punchline: Dan's cello metaphor for flipping someone off — presumably miming an extended middle finger as a 'bow' on a massive instrument
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable jokes. The conceit of flipping someone off being described as playing 'Fuck You' on the world's biggest cello is wonderfully specific and absurdist. It escalates a rude gesture into high culture while simultaneously making it larger and grander. Dan's flair for dramatic self-expression is perfectly captured. Justifies 8+ clearly.

jokes.text: Oh, I think someone just suicide bombed their own future.
jokes.setup: Dan has just dramatically and publicly burned his bridge with Chung
jokes.punchline: Chung's response reframes Dan's self-destruction as a terrorist act against his own career
jokes.explanation: The suicide bomb metaphor for career self-destruction is dark and specific — and from Chung, the military reference is extra appropriate. 'Suicide bombed their own future' is a memorable phrase. Slightly edgy for some but well-deployed.

jokes.text: God, you know, if I had a dollar for every time you mentioned that goddamn war, I would buy a tank and I would blow your fat fucking head off.
jokes.setup: Chung constantly invokes his military service
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'if I had a dollar' construct leads to buying a tank and using it — the escalation is unexpected and the callback to military imagery is precise
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The 'if I had a dollar' cliché is subverted by leading to a tank rather than something mundane, and the callback to Chung's own weapon (a tank — military) to threaten him with is perfectly circular. 'Blow your fat fucking head off' is viscerally funny after the measured setup. The structure is flawless. Justifies 8+ easily and is one of the episode's best.

jokes.text: You get fucked by everybody in DC -- your friends, your enemies, your colleagues, your fucking family. That's Washington, DC, for you. DC -- District of Cunts.
jokes.setup: Selina is venting about being betrayed by everyone around her
jokes.punchline: 'District of Cunts' as the expansion of DC — a perfect Veep coinage
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's signature moments and one of the most quotable lines in the series. 'District of Cunts' is the perfect summary of Veep's worldview — Washington as a place of universal betrayal, compressed into an elegant acronym expansion. The buildup through 'friends, enemies, colleagues, family' (escalating intimacy of betrayers) makes the punchline land with maximum force. Fully justifies scores at the top of the episode's range.

jokes.text: This is a restricted area. We're like Area 51 except more restricted.
jokes.setup: Jonah has brought science fair kids into a restricted area of the White House
jokes.punchline: Jonah's self-important comparison of the VP's office to Area 51, with the added claim that it's MORE restricted — a meaningless escalation
jokes.explanation: Good Jonah joke — his grandiosity applied to the mundane. 'More restricted than Area 51' is empty bragging that reveals his delusion. Consistent character comedy if not exceptional.

jokes.text: I don't know. Information-wise, I am becalmed.
jokes.setup: Selina asks why the president is coming to her office — something unprecedented
jokes.punchline: 'Information-wise, I am becalmed' — a nautical, literary term for being completely stuck, applied to Jonah's ignorance
jokes.explanation: 'Becalmed' is a beautifully unexpected word choice — a nautical term for a ship with no wind. Jonah using this refined vocabulary to describe his ignorance creates a funny class/content mismatch. The structure 'information-wise, I am becalmed' adds bureaucratic formality to the literary term. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: Hey. Hey. / Gary who I'm gonna marry. Fiancée in the house. Ooh, ooh! / This is not a good time, baby.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to turn Dana around before she causes chaos
jokes.punchline: Dana's oblivious entrance, singing her own fiancée status — completely unaware of the political crisis unfolding
jokes.explanation: Dana's unaware cheerfulness crashing into Gary's panic is good comedic contrast. 'Fiancée in the house' is funny for its inappropriateness to the moment.

jokes.text: He should get the basket. Right? We'll get him to tweet about it. It'll be great publicity. You know I'm right.
jokes.setup: Dana learns POTUS is coming and suggests getting him to promote her cheese
jokes.punchline: The idea of the president tweeting about a cheese basket during a constitutional crisis is perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: Dana's collision between the cheese world and political reality reaches maximum absurdity — leveraging a presidential visit for cheese PR. 'You know I'm right' is the perfect clueless confidence.

jokes.text: You've got to mello yellow. / Don't tell me not to panic. I know how to freak out, okay? Everybody can just shut the shit up.
jokes.setup: Selina is spiraling; Andrew tries to calm her
jokes.punchline: 'I know how to freak out, okay?' — Selina's defensive claim of ownership over her panic is absurdly possessive
jokes.explanation: 'I know how to freak out, okay?' is exceptional Selina character comedy. Her insistence on doing panic correctly — asserting competence in incompetence — is both funny and deeply revealing. Only Selina Meyer would claim ownership of her crisis response. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: We got to make a statement. Because I think POTUS is coming here to kick me off the ticket. And I've got to make a statement saying I'm leaving the ticket before he drops me from it. I've got to jump before we get stabbed in the back.
jokes.setup: Selina is in full panic mode, trying to get ahead of POTUS
jokes.punchline: 'I've got to jump before we get stabbed in the back' — a mixed metaphor so wrong it's funny (jumping ≠ avoiding a back-stabbing)
jokes.explanation: 'Jump before we get stabbed in the back' is a great Selina malapropism — she's trying to say 'jump before we're pushed' but adds 'stabbed in the back' creating an impossible physical action. Delivered with total conviction.

jokes.text: I'm gonna run against Chung. Okay? I'm gonna take myself off the ticket. I'll run against Chung and I'll run against POTUS. It'll be like a political massacre.
jokes.setup: Selina is panicking about her political future
jokes.punchline: Her 'solution' escalates to taking on literally everyone simultaneously — a political massacre where she is both perpetrator and most likely victim
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'I'll take myself off the ticket' to 'I'll run against everyone' is funny in its panic logic. Selina's tendency to over-react to crisis by going nuclear is very character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Quit freaking out. You need to get your head together. / What do you mean? The president's gonna be here any second. / Lookie, lookie, lookie, lookie. / This is for you. / What? / It's like a Pecorino peace offering.
jokes.setup: Maximum crisis — POTUS arriving, everyone panicking
jokes.punchline: Gary arrives with a Pecorino peace offering — bringing cheese into a political emergency as a solution
jokes.explanation: 'Pecorino peace offering' during a constitutional crisis is perfectly absurd. Gary's solution to the emotional conflict with Dana is to literally bring cheese into the moment. The cheese thread culminates here beautifully.

jokes.text: Make a hole, yo. POTUS coming through. Make a hole.
jokes.setup: The president is apparently arriving
jokes.punchline: Jonah announces the president with 'Make a hole, yo' — a ridiculous register for such an announcement, mixing military/security speak with 'yo'
jokes.explanation: 'Make a hole, yo' is vintage Jonah — appropriating authority-speak while fundamentally undermining it with 'yo.' The contrast in registers is funny and character-perfect.

jokes.text: T.J.: You're not the president. / No, but you're not Justin Bieber either, are you, sport?
jokes.setup: A science fair kid calls Jonah out for not being the actual president
jokes.punchline: Jonah's comeback is to question the kid's Bieber credentials — a non sequitur that doesn't defend anything
jokes.explanation: A kid destroying Jonah, who then responds with an illogical Justin Bieber non sequitur, is perfectly constructed. The logic 'you're not president = you're not Justin Bieber' is exactly the kind of flailing response Jonah would make, and 'sport' as condescending address to a child who just humiliated him is pitch-perfect. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: Am I about to get whacked?
jokes.setup: Selina is about to enter her own office to meet the president who she fears is about to drop her from the ticket
jokes.punchline: The Mafia vernacular 'whacked' for her political fears — treating it as a gangland hit
jokes.explanation: Good economy — 'whacked' captures both her fear and the Mafia-esque DC power dynamics. Short, effective, character-appropriate.

jokes.text: What's going on here? Is it Bring a Disparate Person to Work Day?
jokes.setup: Chung enters the VP's office to find an odd assortment of people — science kids, cheese vendors, aides
jokes.punchline: 'Bring a Disparate Person to Work Day' — a riff on 'Bring Your Child to Work Day' but for random unconnected people
jokes.explanation: A good Chung observation — his outsider eye captures the absurdity of the office situation. 'Disparate Person' is a specific, official-sounding formulation that's funny. The observation is accurate and well-delivered.

jokes.text: Uh, Selina is about to get a Potal bullet through the head and you are all about to be unemployed and homeless. But, Amy, I don't want you to worry. You can crash at my place.
jokes.setup: Dan dramatically predicts doom for Selina's team
jokes.punchline: Immediately after predicting their homelessness, Dan invites Amy to crash at his place — reframing the apocalypse as a romantic opportunity
jokes.explanation: Classic Dan move — turning everyone's disaster into a personal opportunity. The singling out of Amy from the general doom prediction is sleazy and perfectly timed. 'Potal bullet' (presidential + fatal) is a good neologism. Good character comedy justified at 8.

jokes.text: Take a flare gun, put it in your mouth, and blow your head off.
jokes.setup: Mike keeps bringing up his boat in the middle of a political crisis
jokes.punchline: Amy's response to Mike's boat obsession is a specific, nautical-themed method of self-harm — the flare gun uses the boat imagery against him
jokes.explanation: The callback is what makes this exceptional — Amy uses the boat-specific weapon (flare gun) as the suicide instrument, making the insult perfectly targeted. It's not just 'kill yourself' but a thematically integrated death that uses Mike's own obsession against him. One of the episode's best insult constructions. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Listen to yourselves. You people are monsters.
jokes.setup: The team is arguing and joking during a crisis
jokes.punchline: Ed — the outsider — provides the moral judgment; the comedy is that he's completely right and everyone ignores him
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat — the outsider's accurate judgment goes unheeded. Ed is used well as the normal-person lens but the joke itself is fairly simple.

jokes.text: Sue: Ed, you really need to can it right now. / Do not comment on this office, okay? / Hey, Sue, don't talk to me that way, okay? You're the secretary to the vice president. That's like being Garfunkel's roadie, okay?
jokes.setup: Sue shuts down Ed; Ed fights back
jokes.punchline: 'You're like Garfunkel's roadie' — serving the less important person in an already secondary duo
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The logic is layered: VP is to President as Garfunkel is to Simon, and Sue serves the VP = roadie to Garfunkel. The insult is geometrically constructed and the Paul Simon/Art Garfunkel reference is specific and perfect. Highly quotable. 'Garfunkel's roadie' is a phrase that will stick. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: So this is Selina's team in action. That explains a lot to me. / Likewise. Yeah, well, they're actually better than my team. My team is just fucking horrible.
jokes.setup: Chung and Ben observe the chaos of Selina's team
jokes.punchline: Ben agrees that Selina's chaotic team is actually better than his — then characterizes his own team as 'fucking horrible' with zero self-pity, pure exhausted accuracy
jokes.explanation: Ben's nihilistic self-assessment is perfectly in character. His willingness to throw his own team under the bus with zero defensiveness is funny and revealing. 'Just fucking horrible' delivered with complete calm is peak Ben.

jokes.text: Ma'am, of all the places that I have liaised, I have enjoyed liaising here the most.
jokes.setup: Everyone is celebrating Selina's presidential run announcement (for real this time)
jokes.punchline: Jonah's contribution to the celebration is a bizarrely formal bureaucratic statement about 'liaising' — it's both touching and hilariously inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Jonah trying to be emotional through bureaucratic language is perfect character comedy. He doesn't have the emotional vocabulary for warmth so he says 'liaised' twice. Strangely touching and funny.

jokes.text: POTUS, he knew the gig was up because Doyle, Furlong, Chung all turned the party against him. I mean, it was just, you know, RIP-OTUS. Just complete sinking shit.
jokes.setup: Ben explains why POTUS finally agreed not to run
jokes.punchline: 'RIP-OTUS' — a portmanteau of RIP (Rest in Peace) and POTUS (President of the United States), describing the political death of the president
jokes.explanation: 'RIP-OTUS' is one of the episode's cleverest wordplays. The portmanteau is elegant and immediately comprehensible, and it captures exactly the situation. Ben's delivery of 'complete sinking shit' alongside it adds texture. Highly quotable political coinage. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: And the science fair speech was plagiarized from Chung. Just wanted you to know that up front.
jokes.setup: Everyone is celebrating; Dan joins the celebration
jokes.punchline: Dan drops the plagiarism bomb casually in the middle of the celebration — flagging the disaster he created as an aside
jokes.explanation: Dan casually announcing the plagiarism problem during the victory lap is excellent character comedy and a perfect callback. 'Just wanted you to know that up front' — after nearly half an episode of it being concealed — is beautifully timed. His casual tone for catastrophic news is peak Dan. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: What? What? What? What? / Yep. / You're kidding. / No. / And so it begins. / Yeah, with a kick to the tits.
jokes.setup: The plagiarism revelation lands as Selina begins her presidential campaign
jokes.punchline: 'And so it begins / Yeah, with a kick to the tits' — the perfect summary of this show's worldview: every triumph immediately accompanied by disaster
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. Amy's 'and so it begins' is epic and portentous; Selina's 'yeah, with a kick to the tits' immediately grounds it in her visceral, body-level experience. The exchange perfectly encapsulates Veep's thesis — political ambition as an ongoing cycle of triumph and humiliation. 'A kick to the tits' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. The structure is immaculate. Fully justifies the highest scores.

jokes.text: Who gives a flying fuck? / ( all laugh ) / Not yet. / Sorry. I'm sorry, but I couldn't help it.
jokes.setup: Sue announces the president has left the building
jokes.punchline: Selina's dismissive 'who gives a flying fuck?' — then Gary's premature celebration is corrected ('not yet') because Selina literally can't say that yet politically
jokes.explanation: Two-stage joke: Selina's 'flying fuck' dismissal of POTUS, then Gary literally celebrating (out loud? premature), then Selina's 'not yet' — she can say it privately but not publicly. The room's laughter and then correction is good comic structure.

jokes.text: I didn't arrive soon enough to be able to cut out the cancer. / Oh, no, I think you arrived with a man bag filled with tumors.
jokes.setup: Ed suggests POTUS's problem was that he didn't have good people
jokes.punchline: Amy extends the cancer metaphor to say Ed didn't prevent cancer — he introduced cancer (tumors in a man bag)
jokes.explanation: 'Man bag filled with tumors' is one of the episode's most memorable insult constructions. It extends the cancer metaphor precisely and adds the detail of the man bag — both making Ed seem pretentious (man bag) and pathological (tumors). The metaphor is complete and well-constructed. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Because he relied on those two guys. One a burnt-out loser, the other a conniving robot.
jokes.setup: Ben is diagnosing why POTUS failed
jokes.punchline: Ben describes himself ('burnt-out loser') and Dan ('conniving robot') with complete self-awareness and no defensiveness
jokes.explanation: Ben describing himself as a 'burnt-out loser' with complete equanimity is deeply funny. The perfect self-awareness of the description — and the fact that it's accurate — is the joke. 'Conniving robot' for Dan is also exactly right. Ben's total absence of defensiveness makes this exceptional character comedy. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Oh, hello, kids. / Oh, my God, have you been here this whole time? / M&Ms for the scientists.
jokes.setup: After all the chaos, Jonah is discovered to have been minding the science fair kids in Selina's office this whole time
jokes.punchline: Jonah has been babysitting the science nerds during the entire political crisis and has been feeding them M&Ms
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Jonah has been in the corner with M&Ms and kids while the political fate of the nation was being decided around him is a perfect Jonah moment. The callback to his assignment earlier and the bathos of 'M&Ms for the scientists' is good structural comedy.

jokes.text: I think that the president should only have her staff here. / Or his staff. You know, their staff. I mean, I've never seen -- like that one we just passed, I don't know who that person is. I've never seen that person in my life. No business being here.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving the science kids a West Wing tour and commenting on staffing
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'her... or his... their' self-correction while on a West Wing tour reveals she's mentally trying out the presidency — including catching herself and correcting to 'their' as a cover. Then she dismisses random staffers as having 'no business being here' — in the White House where she doesn't work
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'her/his/their' self-correction is a callback to the 'reelection' slip — she keeps revealing her inner narrative. Her critique of others having 'no business being here' while she herself is a visitor is rich irony. Good character-revealing comedy.

jokes.text: Politics is about people, don't you think? Yeah. It is when you think about it. 'Cause without people, you can't have politics.
jokes.setup: Selina is attempting to give an inspirational impromptu civics lesson to the science fair children.
jokes.punchline: The tautological profundity — 'without people, you can't have politics' — is meaningless when unpacked but delivered with total conviction. It's a politician saying nothing while appearing to say something.
jokes.explanation: This is a precise satire of political rhetoric — the tautological statement dressed as wisdom. 'Without people, you can't have politics' is technically true and completely empty. Selina delivering this to children with genuine conviction is perfect character comedy. Strong character integration: she literally cannot stop performing even when no one of consequence is watching. Earns 8.

jokes.text: Selina did not plagiarize Danny Chung's speech. Mike's already told you that. / No, I am not plagiarizing Mike. I know it's boring, but, hey, I'm boring.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving the same denial Mike already gave about the plagiarism
jokes.punchline: She pre-emptively defends against the accusation of 'plagiarizing' Mike's denial by saying 'I know it's boring, but I'm boring'
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke of potentially plagiarizing her own denial about plagiarism is sharp. 'I know it's boring, but I'm boring' as a self-defense is funny and politically clever — boring = consistent = trustworthy.

jokes.text: We're still together, right? / Ed, I haven't got time. / Time for us or do you mean time as a general concept?
jokes.setup: Ed desperately tries to check on his relationship with Selina amid the chaos
jokes.punchline: Ed interprets Selina's dismissal philosophically — 'time as a general concept' — revealing his anxiety turning into existential crisis
jokes.explanation: 'Time for us or time as a general concept?' is an excellent comic distinction — Ed's anxiety about the relationship collides with his attempt to seem intelligent, producing a philosophical non sequitur. Good economy and the existential framing of romantic rejection is funny.

jokes.text: Was your mom plagiarizing the Bible when she said, 'Oh, God, oh, God'?
jokes.setup: A reporter presses on the speech plagiarism
jokes.punchline: Selina's defense of the plagiarism invokes the reporter's mother having sex (implying the 'oh God' is orgasmic), comparing it to 'plagiarizing' God
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. The construction is extraordinary: it takes a plagiarism defense (everything is borrowed), selects the most inappropriate possible example (someone's mother climaxing and using God's name), and builds a logical syllogism that is simultaneously offensive, irrefutable, and completely inappropriate for a political setting. The logic is air-tight while being maximally outrageous. 'Oh, God, oh, God' as plagiarism of the Bible is genuinely inspired. Only Selina — panicked, fearless, and constitutionally unable to read a room — could say this. Compares favorably to the calibration anchors — this is legitimately 9+ territory. Fully justifies the highest scores in the episode.

jokes.text: The president's chair is a couple inches higher than the rest of the chairs in the Oval Office. And I used to think, 'Oh, my God, that's so ridiculous.' But now I think it makes sense because I think it's a good psychological trick.
jokes.setup: Selina is touring the West Wing with science kids
jokes.punchline: Selina's shift from 'that's ridiculous' to 'that makes sense' about the height-advantage chair reveals she's already thinking like a president — justifying the power games she previously criticized
jokes.explanation: This is a character joke that works on multiple levels — it's funny because Selina literally changed her mind based on power proximity, and revealing because it shows her transformation into someone who justifies power games. The observation about the chair is real (a genuine Washington detail), making the joke grounded. Good character revelation through comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, she loved the cheese. Loved it.
jokes.setup: Gary is on the phone with Dana sorting things out; we're led to assume it's a difficult conversation
jokes.punchline: Dana's response to the whole crisis was satisfaction with the cheese — the Pecorino peace offering worked
jokes.explanation: Good callback resolution to the cheese subplot — Dana's love of the cheese resolves the entire conflict. The bathos of cheese as the solution to a relationship crisis (and an inadvertent political crisis) is satisfying.

jokes.text: When I grow up, I want to be vice president just like you. / Oh, no, you don't. You want to be president.
jokes.setup: A child expresses admiration for Selina as VP
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately corrects the child — you don't want to be VP, you want the real job — revealing her total contempt for the office she holds
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing joke. It encapsulates the entire series — Selina's contempt for the VP job, her presidential ambitions, and her inability to let anyone be satisfied with less than the top. The instinct to correct a child's innocent aspiration upward reveals everything about her. Maximum economy (two lines), maximum character integration (could only be Selina), perfectly structured as episode closer. This belongs among the episode's very best moments. Justifies 8+.

tmdb_overview: Selina and her staff try to cope when the administration goes into full crisis mode.
insight: Rapid-fire wordplay and character escalation sustain 71 jokes across season finale.
